title: Transactions and Locking
description: Transaction control, isolation levels, locking behavior, and deadlock handling

concepts.title: Transaction Control

sqlServer.description: SQL Server uses BEGIN TRANSACTION (or BEGIN TRAN), COMMIT TRANSACTION (or COMMIT TRAN), and ROLLBACK TRANSACTION (or ROLLBACK TRAN). Savepoints are created with SAVE TRANSACTION <name> and rolled back to with ROLLBACK TRANSACTION <name>. Note that rolling back to a savepoint does not end the transaction — a final COMMIT or ROLLBACK is still required. @@ERROR checks the error state after each statement.
notes: BEGIN TRAN is valid shorthand for BEGIN TRANSACTION
ROLLBACK TRANSACTION <SavepointName> only rolls back to the savepoint, not the full transaction
@@ERROR is reset after every statement; use TRY/CATCH for robust error handling
SQL Server supports named transactions: BEGIN TRANSACTION MyTxn

postgres.description: PostgreSQL uses BEGIN (or BEGIN TRANSACTION / START TRANSACTION), COMMIT, and ROLLBACK. Savepoints use SAVEPOINT <name>, ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT <name>, and optionally RELEASE SAVEPOINT <name>. PostgreSQL's syntax is cleaner and more standard-SQL compliant. Error handling within transactions is done via the application layer or PL/pgSQL exception blocks.
keyDifferences: PostgreSQL uses SAVEPOINT / ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT instead of SQL Server's SAVE TRANSACTION / ROLLBACK TRANSACTION <name>
PostgreSQL uses BEGIN or START TRANSACTION; SQL Server uses BEGIN TRANSACTION or BEGIN TRAN
PostgreSQL has RELEASE SAVEPOINT to free the savepoint without rolling back; SQL Server has no equivalent
@@ERROR checking in SQL Server is statement-level; PostgreSQL relies on PL/pgSQL EXCEPTION blocks for programmatic error handling
notes: START TRANSACTION is an alias for BEGIN in PostgreSQL
Rolling back to a SAVEPOINT does not end the transaction — COMMIT or ROLLBACK still required
RELEASE SAVEPOINT removes the savepoint marker but keeps all work done since then
Autonomous transactions (inner transactions independent of outer) are not supported in PostgreSQL

concepts.keyDifference: SQL Server uses SAVE TRANSACTION / ROLLBACK TRANSACTION <name> for savepoints; PostgreSQL uses SAVEPOINT / ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT — standard SQL syntax that is cleaner and more portable.

concepts.title: Isolation Levels

sqlServer.description: SQL Server supports five isolation levels: READ UNCOMMITTED (dirty reads allowed), READ COMMITTED (default, no dirty reads), REPEATABLE READ (no non-repeatable reads), SERIALIZABLE (no phantom reads), and SNAPSHOT (row versioning, requires database configuration). Set using SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL before starting a transaction. The level persists for the session until changed.
notes: READ UNCOMMITTED allows dirty reads — reading uncommitted data from other transactions
SNAPSHOT isolation requires ALTER DATABASE ... SET ALLOW_SNAPSHOT_ISOLATION ON
READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT (RCSI) is a separate database-level setting that changes how READ COMMITTED works
Isolation level can be checked via sys.dm_exec_sessions for the current session

postgres.description: PostgreSQL supports four isolation levels via its MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) architecture: READ UNCOMMITTED (silently upgraded to READ COMMITTED), READ COMMITTED (default), REPEATABLE READ, and SERIALIZABLE. Isolation level is set per transaction using BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL <level> or SET default_transaction_isolation for the session. PostgreSQL's REPEATABLE READ also prevents phantom reads unlike the SQL standard definition.
keyDifferences: READ UNCOMMITTED does not exist in practice in PostgreSQL — MVCC makes dirty reads impossible, so it behaves as READ COMMITTED
PostgreSQL has no SNAPSHOT isolation level — READ COMMITTED already uses MVCC snapshots by default
PostgreSQL REPEATABLE READ prevents phantom reads (stricter than the SQL standard requires)
PostgreSQL SERIALIZABLE uses SSI (Serializable Snapshot Isolation) — no blocking, but may abort transactions with serialization failures
Isolation level is set per-transaction in PostgreSQL, not per-session (though session default can be changed with SET)
notes: MVCC means readers never block writers and writers never block readers in PostgreSQL
Serialization failures return SQLSTATE 40001 — applications should retry these transactions
PostgreSQL's REPEATABLE READ is stronger than the SQL standard: it prevents phantom reads too
No ALLOW_SNAPSHOT_ISOLATION configuration needed — PostgreSQL always uses MVCC

concepts.keyDifference: PostgreSQL uses MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) making dirty reads impossible — READ UNCOMMITTED behaves as READ COMMITTED. There is no SNAPSHOT isolation level because all reads are already MVCC-based. PostgreSQL SERIALIZABLE uses non-blocking Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) instead of SQL Server's lock-based approach.

concepts.title: Locking Hints

sqlServer.description: SQL Server supports table hints that override the query optimizer's default locking strategy. WITH (NOLOCK) is commonly used to avoid blocking on reads but risks dirty reads. WITH (UPDLOCK) is used to prevent deadlocks when a SELECT will be followed by an UPDATE. WITH (HOLDLOCK) escalates to a serializable lock. WITH (ROWLOCK) requests row-level granularity. Hints are applied per-table in the FROM clause.
notes: WITH (NOLOCK) is equivalent to READUNCOMMITTED and can return uncommitted, rolled-back, or duplicate rows
WITH (UPDLOCK) is the recommended pattern for read-then-update operations to prevent deadlocks
WITH (HOLDLOCK) is equivalent to WITH (SERIALIZABLE) for that specific table
Lock hints are hints — SQL Server may escalate locks regardless

postgres.description: PostgreSQL provides row-level locking through SELECT ... FOR UPDATE, FOR SHARE, FOR NO KEY UPDATE, and FOR KEY SHARE clauses. These are applied in the SELECT statement's locking clause, not as table hints. FOR UPDATE is the most common — it prevents other transactions from locking or modifying selected rows. SKIP LOCKED and NOWAIT modifiers control behavior when rows are already locked. There is no NOLOCK equivalent because PostgreSQL's MVCC architecture eliminates dirty reads without needing a lock hint.
keyDifferences: No WITH (NOLOCK) equivalent — PostgreSQL MVCC means readers never see uncommitted data without any hint needed
Locking clauses are part of the SELECT statement (FOR UPDATE / FOR SHARE), not table hints
PostgreSQL adds SKIP LOCKED and NOWAIT modifiers not available in SQL Server table hints
FOR NO KEY UPDATE and FOR KEY SHARE are PostgreSQL-specific granular lock modes for FK-safe locking
PostgreSQL has no page-level or table-level lock hints in SELECT — use LOCK TABLE for explicit table locks
notes: SKIP LOCKED is excellent for implementing job queues — each worker grabs unlocked rows
NOWAIT raises an error immediately instead of waiting for locked rows
FOR KEY SHARE is acquired automatically by FK constraint checks — use FOR NO KEY UPDATE to avoid blocking FK lookups
LOCK TABLE accounts IN SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE MODE provides table-level locking in PostgreSQL

concepts.keyDifference: SQL Server's WITH (NOLOCK) hint has no PostgreSQL equivalent — MVCC makes dirty reads impossible by design, so no hint is needed. PostgreSQL uses SELECT ... FOR UPDATE/FOR SHARE locking clauses instead of table hints, and adds powerful SKIP LOCKED and NOWAIT options for queue processing patterns.

concepts.title: Deadlock Detection and Handling

sqlServer.description: SQL Server automatically detects deadlocks and chooses one transaction as the 'deadlock victim', rolling it back and raising error 1205. Applications should catch error 1205 in a BEGIN CATCH block and implement retry logic. The DEADLOCK_PRIORITY setting can influence which transaction becomes the victim. Use sys.dm_exec_requests to monitor blocking sessions.
notes: SQL Server's deadlock monitor runs every 5 seconds by default (configurable)
Use SET DEADLOCK_PRIORITY LOW to make your session the preferred deadlock victim
Deadlock graphs can be captured via Extended Events or SQL Server Profiler
@@TRANCOUNT must be checked before ROLLBACK in CATCH blocks — nested transactions affect this count

postgres.code:
-- PostgreSQL deadlock retry pattern using PL/pgSQL
-- SQLSTATE 40P01 = deadlock detected

DO $$
DECLARE
    v_retry_count INT := 0;
    v_max_retries INT := 3;
    v_success BOOLEAN := FALSE;
BEGIN
    WHILE v_retry_count < v_max_retries AND NOT v_success LOOP
        BEGIN
            UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 10.00 WHERE account_id = 1;
            v_success := TRUE;
        EXCEPTION
            WHEN deadlock_detected THEN  -- SQLSTATE 40P01
                v_retry_count := v_retry_count + 1;
                PERFORM pg_sleep(0.1);  -- Wait 100ms before retry
            WHEN OTHERS THEN
                RAISE;  -- Re-raise non-deadlock errors
        END;
    END LOOP;
END;
$$;

-- Query active connections and blocking info
SELECT
    pid,
    usename,
    application_name,
    state,
    wait_event_type,
    wait_event,
    LEFT(query, 100) AS query_snippet
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state != 'idle'
ORDER BY pid;

-- Find blocking locks
SELECT
    blocked.pid AS blocked_pid,
    blocking.pid AS blocking_pid,
    blocked.query AS blocked_query,
    blocking.query AS blocking_query
FROM pg_stat_activity blocked
JOIN pg_stat_activity blocking
    ON blocking.pid = ANY(pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid))
WHERE cardinality(pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid)) > 0;
postgres.description: PostgreSQL automatically detects deadlocks and aborts one of the involved transactions, raising SQLSTATE 40P01 (deadlock_detected). Retry logic is implemented in PL/pgSQL using an EXCEPTION WHEN deadlock_detected block. In application code, check for SQLSTATE 40P01 and retry the transaction. Use pg_stat_activity and pg_blocking_pids() to monitor blocking. Unlike SQL Server, PostgreSQL has no DEADLOCK_PRIORITY setting — the victim is chosen by internal heuristics.
keyDifferences: PostgreSQL uses SQLSTATE 40P01 (deadlock_detected) instead of SQL Server error number 1205
Retry logic in PostgreSQL uses PL/pgSQL EXCEPTION WHEN deadlock_detected blocks, not TRY/CATCH
pg_stat_activity + pg_blocking_pids() replaces sys.dm_exec_requests for monitoring blocking
PostgreSQL has no DEADLOCK_PRIORITY setting — deadlock victim selection is automatic
In PostgreSQL, a deadlock aborts only the current transaction; the connection remains open unlike some errors
notes: SQLSTATE 40001 (serialization_failure) is also retriable — occurs with SERIALIZABLE isolation level
Applications should implement retry logic at the connection layer, not just in stored procedures
deadlock_timeout GUC parameter controls how long PostgreSQL waits before checking for deadlocks (default 1s)
pg_blocking_pids(pid) returns an array of PIDs blocking the given session — useful for lock monitoring

concepts.keyDifference: SQL Server raises error number 1205 caught via ERROR_NUMBER() in BEGIN CATCH; PostgreSQL raises SQLSTATE 40P01 (deadlock_detected) caught via EXCEPTION WHEN deadlock_detected in PL/pgSQL. Monitor blocking with sys.dm_exec_requests (SQL Server) vs pg_stat_activity + pg_blocking_pids() (PostgreSQL).
